text: sharing the case of a right colectomy for a patient with right colon polyposis and large cecal polyps that were found initially two years ago. Patient was counseled for surgery. by his gastroenterologist and another surgeon, but because he had multiple prior operations, including two X-lapsed through a Chevron incision for trauma, and one was a thoracal abdominal on the left side and an open common bile duct exploration on the right side, he was told he could only have an open operation. And believe it or not, challenged the patient to decide not even to have surgery at the time that the biopsies of the colon polyps were all benign. Cut to two years later, he has another colonoscopy and now the cecal polyp is a cancer.

The surgeon begins the procedure with a left parumbilical incision for camera placement using a 12mm balloon trocar, along with a right lower quadrant 5mm port and a suprapubic 12mm port. The surgeon is taking down omental adhesions from the anterior abdominal wall to locate the transverse colon, which is marked with a tattoo distal to the colonic polyposis. The camera is positioned in the suprapubic port, revealing small bowel adhesions in the left upper quadrant. The surgeon continues to remove omental adhesions and starts to visualize the colon.

The surgeon reduces the hernia through the right side of the Chevron incision, revealing diastatic changes to the anterior abdominal wall. Adhesions of the omentum are visible, and the transverse colon is plastered to the incision site. The surgeon works to mobilize the colon for an anastomosis and locate the tattoo marking the distal extent of the right-sided colon polyposis.

The surgeon observes multiple hernias through the incision and searches for the tattoo marking the distal extent of the colon polyposis. The colon is adhered to the liver bed, complicating the search for the tattoo. The surgeon attempts to set up a medial to lateral approach, moving the small bowel aside, and notices a faint tattoo in the mesentery while trying to tent the ileocolic pedicle.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the right colon to elevate the ileocolic pedicle, using an inferior approach to mobilize the cecum. The camera is positioned in the suprapubic port, with working instruments in the left lower quadrant and left parambulical port.

The surgeon continues the dissection around the iliocolic pedicle, aiming to locate the duodenum and navigate around the hepatic flexure. The duodenum becomes visible as the surgeon works to mobilize the colon, which is adhered to the gallbladder fossa. Efforts are made to detach the colon from the abdominal wall and liver bed.

The camera is positioned in the suprapubic port, with working instruments in the left lower quadrant and left parambulical port. The surgeon attempts to locate the transverse colon and its tattoo, noting the stomach and tattoo in the mesentery but not yet visible in the bowel wall. The surgeon proceeds to take down the flexure, aiming to detach the colon from the liver edge, with the lateral dissection intended to improve mesentery elevation.

The surgeon navigates around the hepatic flexure, joining the previous lateral dissection to expose the colon. The tattoo marking the distal margin is visible several centimeters distal to the last colon polyp on the right side. The surgeon works to free the colon from the liver edge and mobilizes further distally to prepare for anastomosis.
The surgeon attempts to lift the ileocolic pedicle after completing lateral dissection, using sponges to hold surrounding tissues. A window is created around the base of the ileocolic pedicle.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic pedicle and proceeds with dissection, achieving a safe ligation and removal of thirty-two lymph nodes. The dissection continues towards the right branch of the middle colic artery, approaching the tattooed colon margin. The omentum is split at the distal margin level and taken en bloc with the specimen.

The surgeon takes the remaining small bowel mesentery and uses ICG to visualize as they staple the margins. The stapler is introduced through the suprapubic port to take the small bowel. The specimen is temporarily placed above the liver. The surgeon prepares for an isoparastaltic intracorporeal anastomosis by making a colotomy and bringing in the stapler with a vascular load for the common channel.

The surgeon observes a clear open lumen with no bleeding and proceeds to suture the common channel shut. Using two needle drivers, the surgeon alternates between right and left hands to take suturing bites, adapting to the angles for optimal technique.

The surgeon switches back to right-handed suturing to perform a continuous suture along the cumular neurotomy, using a 3-0 V-lock suture. The suture is run up and then reinforced back down, starting at the near corner to prevent inward rolling. Clips are applied to secure the suture, and the anastomosis is covered with omentum. The specimen is retrieved through a fan-and-steel incision at the port site, with visible adhesions left intact.
